import numpy as np

# Constants
rho = 1.225 # Density of air in kg/m^3
g = 9.81 # Acceleration due to gravity in m/s^2

# Aircraft parameters
b = 30 # Wing span in meters
S = 80 # Wing area in square meters
AR = 10 # Aspect ratio
W = 100000 # Maximum takeoff weight in kg
Tmax = 50000 # Maximum thrust in N
Clmax = 1.5 # Maximum lift coefficient
alpha\_max = np.radians(15) # Maximum angle of attack in radians
x\_cg = 0.25 # Center of gravity location as a fraction of mean aerodynamic chord

# Calculate mean aerodynamic chord (MAC)
MAC = S / b

# Calculate reference area and reference length
Sref = S
Lref = MAC

# Define state variables
# x = [u, v, w, p, q, r, phi, theta, psi, x\_E, y\_E, z\_E, alpha, beta]
# u: velocity in x direction (body axis)
# v: velocity in y direction (body axis)
# w: velocity in z direction (body axis)
# p: roll rate
# q: pitch rate
# r: yaw rate
# phi: roll angle
# theta: pitch angle
# psi: yaw angle
# x\_E: x coordinate in Earth frame
# y\_E: y coordinate in Earth frame
# z\_E: z coordinate in Earth frame
# alpha: angle of attack
# beta: sideslip angle

# Define control inputs
# delta\_e: elevator deflection
# delta\_a: aileron deflection
# delta\_r: rudder deflection
# delta\_t: throttle setting

# Define state equations (linearized around trim condition)
def state\_eqns(t, x, delta\_e, delta\_a, delta\_r, delta\_t):
 u, v, w, p, q, r, phi, theta, psi, x\_E, y\_E, z\_E, alpha, beta = x
 
 # Calculate forces and moments
 V = np.sqrt(u\*\*2 + v\*\*2 + w\*\*2) # Airspeed
 qbar = 0.5 \* rho \* V\*\*2 # Dynamic pressure
 alpha = np.arctan(w/u) # Angle of attack
 beta = np.arcsin(v/V) # Sideslip angle
 CL = Clmax \* alpha / alpha\_max # Lift coefficient
 CD = 0.1 \* CL\*\*2 + 0.05 # Drag coefficient (assumed)
 L = qbar \* Sref \* CL # Lift force
 D = qbar \* Sref \* CD # Drag force
 Thrust = delta\_t \* Tmax - D # Thrust force
 Tq = 0.0 # Tor

Title : # Robots, Extinction, and Salvation: On Altruism in Human–Posthuman Interactions
Summary : Posthumanism and transhumanism are philosophies that envision possible relations between humans and posthumans. Critical versions of posthumanism and transhumanism examine the idea of potential threats involved in human–posthuman interactions (i.e., species extinction, species domination, AI takeover) and propose precautionary measures against these threats by elaborating protocols for the prosocial use of technology. Critics of these philosophies usually argue against the reality of the threats or dispute the feasibility of the proposed measures. We take this debate back to its modern roots. The play that gave the world the term “robot” (R.U.R.: Rossum’s Universal Robots) is nowadays remembered mostly as a particular instance of an absurd apocalyptic vision about the doom of the human species through technology. However, we demonstrate that Karel Capek assumed that a negative interpretation of human–posthuman interactions emerges mainly from the human inability to think clearly about extinction, spirituality, and technology. We propose that the conflictual interpretation of human–posthuman interactions can be overcome by embracing Capek’s religiously and philosophically-inspired theory of altruism remediated by technology. We argue that this reinterpretation of altruism may strengthen the case for a more positive outlook on human–posthuman interactions.

题目：Walmart: inflation oration
LEX., Financial Times, 17 November 2021
Who’s afraid of the big, bad price rise? Not Americans. US inflation may have hit a 31-year high in October. But consumers are still eager to spend.
This should be good news for Walmart. The world’s biggest bricks-and-mortar retailer reported strong third-quarter sales and raised its full-year outlook. Supply-chain disruptions and fears of empty shelves are prompting many Americans to get a head start on their holiday shopping.
Do not be fooled by the topline growth. There are reasons that shares in Walmart have gone nowhere this year. The stock is down 3 per cent, compared with the 48 per cent gains at rivals Target and Home Depot.
To ensure that its shelves are stocked for the holidays, Walmart has taken drastic measures, including using air freight and chartering its own cargo ships. This has helped it to increase US inventory 11.5 per cent in the quarter.
That has come at a cost. Gross quarterly margins fell 42 basis points to 24.6 per cent. This compares with 34.1 per cent at Home Depot and the 30 per cent posted by Target.
Pressure will persist. Labour shortages mean low-wage Walmart will need to pay up for workers. Home Depot sells bulky items that cannot be readily ordered from Amazon. Target has strong in-house brands that help protect margins. But Walmart is more exposed to the inflationary squeeze.
Many of the products it carries can readily be bought elsewhere.
Walmart still trades at a premium to Target, which is on 21 times forward earnings. But the
valuation has fallen from 27 to 23 times over the past year.
To break out of rangebound trading, Walmart will have to find new sources of growth. Potential
catalysts include Walmart’s ad unit and its marketplace business where third-party vendors sell their wares. Walmart, once written off as a doomed physical retailer, has staged a remarkable recovery. The era of rising inflation means another reinvention is required.

\item {\bf Polar Transformation:} Given integers $i, j \in \{0,\ldots,2^{\ell}-1\}$ we say $i \preceq j$ if every digit in the binary representation of $i$ is at most the corresponding digit in the binary representation of $j$. Formally if $i = \sum\_{k=0}^{\ell-1} i\_k 2^k$ and $j = \sum\_{k=0}^{\ell-1} j\_k 2^k$ where $i\_0,\ldots,i\_{\ell-1},j\_0,\ldots,j\_{\ell-1} \in \{0,1\}$, then $i \preceq j$ if and only if $i\_k \leq j\_k$ for every $k \in \{0,\ldots,\ell-1\}$. For bits $a,b \in \{0,1\}$ let $a \oplus b$ denote their XOR or their sum modulo 2. (So $a\oplus b = 1$ iff $a \ne b$.) 

For $n = 2^{\ell}$ the Polar Transformation $P\_n$ transforms $n$ bits to $n$ bits as follows: $P\_n(x\_0,\ldots,x\_{n-1}) = (w\_0,\ldots,w\_{n-1})$ where
$$w\_j = \bigoplus\_{i \preceq j} x\_i.$$ (For instance, $w\_{20} = x\_{20} \oplus x\_{16} \oplus x\_4 \oplus x\_0$.) 
In 2008, Erdal Arikan proved that the Polar Transformation can be used to extract an asymptotically optimal number of ``nearly'' unbiased coin tosses from a collection of biased coins. Specifically, if the $x\_i$s are outputs of $n$ coins of known bias $p$, then there is a subset $S \subseteq \{0,\ldots,2^{\ell}-1\}$ with $|S| \approx A(p)\cdot n$, where $A(p)$ is the quantity defined in Lecture 1, such that $\{w\_i\}\_{i\in S}$ are nearly unbiased coins. (The actual definition of ``nearly unbiased'' is omitted in this description, and both it and the background starting ``In 2008'' are not needed to solve the problem.) 

\begin{enumerate}
 \item 
 {\bf (15 points)}

from functools import reduce
from gensim.models import Word2Vec
from nltk.tokenize import word\_tokenize
from nltk.corpus import stopwords
from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer
from nltk import pos\_tag, ne\_chunk

def preprocess\_text(text):
 # Tokenize the text
 tokens = word\_tokenize(text)
 # Remove stopwords
 stop\_words = set(stopwords.words("english"))
 filtered\_tokens = list(filter(lambda token: token.lower() not in stop\_words, tokens))
 # POS tagging
 tagged\_tokens = pos\_tag(filtered\_tokens)
 # Lemmatize using POS tagging
 lemmatizer = WordNetLemmatizer()
 lemmatized\_tokens = list(map(lambda token\_tag: lemmatizer.lemmatize(token\_tag[0], pos=pos\_mapping(token\_tag[1])) if token\_tag[1].startswith(("NN", "VB", "JJ")) else lemmatizer.lemmatize(token\_tag[0]),tagged\_tokens))
 # NER chunking
 ner\_tree = ne\_chunk(tagged\_tokens)
 named\_entities = list(reduce(lambda named\_entities, subtree: named\_entities + [(subtree.label(), ' '.join(c[0] for c in subtree))] if hasattr(subtree, 'label') else named\_entities, ner\_tree, []))
 # train the model
 model = Word2Vec([lemmatized\_tokens], min\_count=1)
 return model, named\_entities

def pos\_mapping(tag):
 if tag.startswith("NN"):
 return 'n'
 elif tag.startswith("VB"):
 return 'v'
 elif tag.startswith("JJ"):
 return 'a'
 else:
 return None

def print\_word2vec\_model\_summary(model):
 print("Embedding size:", model.vector\_size)
 print("Vocab size:", len(model.wv.index\_to\_key))

def main(text):
 model, named\_entities = preprocess\_text(text)
 print\_word2vec\_model\_summary(model)
 # check the vocabulary
 print(model.wv.index\_to\_key)
 # check the similarity between words
 print(model.wv.similarity('sentence', 'first'))
 # check named entities
 print(named\_entities)

 self, tenant\_id: str, project\_type: str, request\_id: str
 ) -> str:
 """
 Retrieves the project ID for the specified tenant and project type.

 Args:
 tenant\_id (str): The ID of the tenant.
 project\_type (str): The type of the project.
 request\_id (str): The request-id for distributed tracing.
 Returns:
 str: The ID of the project.

 Raises:
 InternalServerError: If an error occurs during the operation.
 """
 try:
 project\_db: ProjectDB = await self.queries.get\_by\_tenant\_id(
 ProjectDB, tenant\_id, project\_type
 )
 project\_id = project\_db.project\_id
 return project\_id
 except Exception as exc:
 msg = "Error getting project"
 log\_exception(
 message=msg,
 error=exc,
 request\_id=request\_id,
 tenant\_id=tenant\_id,
 )
 raise InternalServerError(
 f"{msg} for tenant {tenant\_id}: {exc}"
 ) from exc
